title: Oath: Chronicles of Empire and Exile

walkthrough.title: Unpack the Chronicle
walkthrough.text: Let's start setting up Oath. If this is your very first game, grab the Chronicle of the game from the box. This is a special sheet that records the state of the world between games. For a first play, we will use the default setup printed in the rulebook, so set the Chronicle aside for now.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Map Board
walkthrough.text: Place the large map board in the center of the table. It shows three regions from left to right: the Cradle, the Provinces, and the Hinterland. Each region has slots for site cards. The Cradle is the heart of the empire and is where the Chancellor begins.

walkthrough.title: Place the Site Cards
walkthrough.text: For a first game, take the eight starting site cards listed in the rulebook and place them face up in the site slots across the three regions. Each site has a name and a capacity that determines how many denizen cards it can hold. In future games, which sites appear will depend on the previous game's outcome.

walkthrough.title: Build the World Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's assemble the world deck. For a first game, use the default deck composition listed in the setup guide. Sort denizen cards by suit to find the ones specified, then shuffle them all together into one face-down deck. This deck represents the people, places, and powers that exist in the world.

walkthrough.title: Deal Denizens to Sites
walkthrough.text: Draw denizen cards from the top of the world deck and place them face up onto the site slots across the map. Each site holds a specific number of cards based on its capacity. Fill every site slot so the entire map is populated. These denizens are available for players to interact with during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reliquary
walkthrough.text: Find the reliquary board and the relic cards. For a first game, place the starting relics face down in the reliquary slots as directed by the setup guide. Relics are powerful unique items that players can discover by searching the world deck. The reliquary persists between games.

walkthrough.title: Place the Oath Card
walkthrough.text: Take the oath card and set it near the map where everyone can see it. For a first game, use the Oath of Supremacy, which means the Chancellor wins by controlling the most sites. In future games, the oath will be determined by the previous winner. The oath defines the primary victory condition for the Chancellor and the default win condition.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Banners
walkthrough.text: Place the banner tokens near the board. There are two key banners: the People's Favor and the Darkest Secret. These banners move between players during the game based on who has the most influence in the corresponding suit or the most secrets. Banners are critical for certain victory conditions.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Favor and Secret Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place all favor tokens and secret tokens in separate supply piles near the board. Favor is the main currency used for trading and interacting with denizens. Secrets are a hidden resource with powerful uses. Both will be distributed to players during role setup.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Chancellor
walkthrough.text: One player must be the Chancellor, the ruler of the empire. For a first game, give this role to the most experienced player, or choose randomly. The Chancellor has a different starting position and different goals than everyone else. Hand the Chancellor their player board and pawn.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Chancellor
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor places their pawn in the Cradle region. They take warbands from the supply and place them on sites in the Cradle and Provinces as directed by the setup guide. The Chancellor also takes their starting favor and secret tokens. The Chancellor begins with more resources and territory than anyone else.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Exiles
walkthrough.text: All other players are Exiles. Each Exile takes their player board, pawn, and warbands in their chosen color. Exiles place their pawns on sites at the edges of the map, typically in the Hinterland or outer Provinces. Each Exile receives a smaller allotment of starting favor and secrets as indicated in the setup guide.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Warband Supplies
walkthrough.text: Each player has a limited supply of warbands. Place any warbands not yet on the board onto your player board in the supply area. Your supply is public information. You can never have more warbands than your supply allows, so managing them carefully throughout the game is essential.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Vision Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the vision cards, which are a separate small deck. These provide alternative win conditions for Exiles. Shuffle them and place the deck face down near the world deck. Exiles can search for vision cards during the game to give themselves a unique path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on space one of the round track. The game lasts up to eight rounds. Victory conditions begin being checked at the end of round five, so you have four rounds to build your position before anyone can win. The Chancellor always takes the first turn each round.

walkthrough.title: Review the Starting Map
walkthrough.text: Let's take a moment to look at the board together. The Cradle is the Chancellor's stronghold with warbands defending it. The Provinces are contested middle ground. The Hinterland is where the Exiles start. Denizens fill the sites, the world deck is ready, and the oath is visible. Everyone should now understand the geography before we begin.

walkthrough.title: The Core Conflict
walkthrough.text: Oath is a game about power. One player is the Chancellor, defending the empire's current order. Everyone else starts as an Exile, trying to overthrow the Chancellor or find an alternative path to victory. The twist is that each game's outcome shapes the next game's world, creating an evolving story across multiple plays.

walkthrough.title: Rounds and Turns
walkthrough.text: The game lasts up to eight rounds. Each round, every player takes one turn, starting with the Chancellor and going clockwise. On your turn, you get a limited number of supply, then spend it to take actions. The game can end as early as round five, when victory conditions start being checked at the end of each round.

walkthrough.title: Supply Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, you gain supply. This means you add warbands and collect favor based on the sites you control and the cards in your advisers column. Supply is your fuel for the entire turn. If you control more sites and have more powerful advisers, you get more supply to work with.

walkthrough.title: Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you may take multiple actions by spending supply. The six main actions are Travel, Muster, Trade, Campaign, Search, and Recover. Each action costs supply to perform. You can take the same action multiple times if you have the supply for it. Let's walk through each action in detail.

walkthrough.title: Action: Travel
walkthrough.text: Travel lets you move your pawn from one site to another on the map. Moving costs supply, and the cost depends on how far you travel. You can move to any site on the map, but moving to distant regions costs more. Your pawn's location determines where you can muster, trade, campaign, and search.

walkthrough.title: Action: Muster
walkthrough.text: Muster lets you place warbands from your supply onto the site where your pawn is located. Warbands represent your military presence on the map. Having more warbands on a site helps you control it and defend against attacks. You need supply to muster, so plan your warband placement carefully.

walkthrough.title: Action: Trade
walkthrough.text: Trade lets you interact with denizen cards at your current site. You can take a face-up denizen from the site and add it to your advisers, paying its favor cost. Advisers provide ongoing powers and boost your supply. You can also use Trade to exchange favor for secrets or interact with the economy in other ways.

walkthrough.title: Advisers and Their Powers
walkthrough.text: When you trade for a denizen, it becomes one of your advisers. Advisers sit in a column on your player board and provide various benefits. Some grant extra supply at the start of your turn. Others give combat bonuses, special abilities, or suit influence. Building a strong adviser column is one of the most important things you can do in the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Campaign
walkthrough.text: Campaign is how you fight. Declare an attack on warbands at your site or an adjacent site. Both the attacker and defender roll dice based on their warband count. Compare results to determine casualties. If you eliminate all defending warbands, you take control of the site. Campaigns are expensive and risky, but they are sometimes the only way to seize power.

walkthrough.title: Combat Resolution
walkthrough.text: When you campaign, the attacker rolls attack dice and the defender rolls defense dice. Each hit symbol removes one opposing warband. The defender has the advantage of being dug in. Some adviser powers grant bonus dice or modify combat results. After combat, any remaining warbands stay on the site, and control may shift if the attacker eliminated all defenders.

walkthrough.title: Action: Search
walkthrough.text: Search lets you draw cards from the world deck. When you search, you draw one or more cards and choose what to do with them. You might find denizens you can play to a site or take as advisers. You might find relics, which are powerful unique items. Or you might find a vision card, which gives an Exile an alternative win condition.

walkthrough.title: Action: Recover
walkthrough.text: Recover lets you regain resources and refresh your position. You can recover spent favor, return used warbands to your supply, or retrieve cards. This action is about resetting for your next big move. Do not underestimate Recover, as running out of supply at a critical moment can cost you the game.

walkthrough.title: Site Control
walkthrough.text: You control a site if you have the most warbands there. Controlling sites is crucial because it determines your supply income, fulfills certain oath conditions, and grants you power over the denizens at that location. The Chancellor starts controlling several sites, and Exiles must fight to take them.

walkthrough.title: The Six Denizen Suits
walkthrough.text: Denizen cards belong to one of six suits: Discord, Arcane, Order, Hearth, Beast, and Nomad. Each suit has a thematic flavor and typical card powers. Suit influence determines who holds the People's Favor banner. The composition of suits in the world deck evolves between games, shaping available strategies.

walkthrough.title: Banners: People's Favor
walkthrough.text: The People's Favor banner goes to the player with the most influence in the most popular suit. This is tracked by counting suited cards among your advisers and controlled sites. Holding People's Favor can be a win condition depending on the oath. It can shift between players as advisers are gained and lost.

walkthrough.title: Banners: Darkest Secret
walkthrough.text: The Darkest Secret banner goes to the player holding the most secret tokens. Secrets are gained through specific actions and card powers. Like People's Favor, the Darkest Secret is a potential win condition. Accumulating secrets while keeping others from doing so is a viable path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Citizenship
walkthrough.text: An Exile can offer to become a Citizen by swearing allegiance to the Chancellor. The Chancellor may accept or reject the offer. If accepted, the Citizen shares in the Chancellor's victory if the Chancellor wins. Citizens gain access to the Cradle and can benefit from the Chancellor's power. However, a Citizen can later betray the Chancellor and return to Exile status.

walkthrough.title: Vision Cards
walkthrough.text: These provide Exiles with alternative win conditions beyond seizing the oath. When an Exile plays a vision card, it becomes public knowledge. The vision specifies conditions the Exile must fulfill, such as controlling specific sites or holding certain relics. Other players can see your vision and try to block it.

walkthrough.title: The Oath
walkthrough.text: Card defines the Chancellor's primary win condition. There are four oath types: Supremacy requires controlling the most sites, the People requires holding the People's Favor banner, Devotion requires holding the Darkest Secret banner, and Protection requires a specific defensive condition. The Chancellor wins by holding the oath condition at the end of rounds five through eight.

walkthrough.title: Victory Conditions
walkthrough.text: Starting at the end of round five, victory is checked each round. The Chancellor wins by fulfilling the oath condition. An Exile with a played vision card wins by fulfilling that vision's condition. Citizens win if the Chancellor wins. If nobody wins by the end of round eight, the Chancellor wins by default. Only one player or alliance can win each game.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation and Deals
walkthrough.text: Oath is a deeply political game. Players can negotiate freely: offering truces, promising future support, threatening attacks, or bargaining with resources. No deal is binding, which means betrayal is always a possibility. The social dynamics at the table are just as important as the mechanical actions you take.

walkthrough.title: The Chronicle: Game-to-Game Persistence
walkthrough.text: The signature feature of Oath is that each game shapes the next. The winner of a game determines the oath for the following game, curates which cards remain in the world deck, and sets the map's starting configuration. Nothing is permanently destroyed or marked. All persistence is tracked through card arrangement and the Chronicle sheet.

walkthrough.title: Becoming the New Chancellor
walkthrough.text: The winner of each game becomes the Chancellor in the next game, inheriting the empire they helped create. If a Citizen wins alongside the Chancellor, one of them takes the role next game. This creates a meta-narrative across your play group's campaign where the world's history is shaped by player actions.

walkthrough.title: World Deck Curation
walkthrough.text: After a game ends, the winner curates the world deck for the next game. They choose which denizen cards to keep and which to remove, shaping what strategies will be available in future games. If Beast cards dominated this game, the winner might remove some to change the landscape. This curation is one of the most powerful aspects of winning.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Empire
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several rounds of a four-player game. One player is the Chancellor, and the other three are Exiles. We will follow scripted moves that demonstrate the core actions and common beginner mistakes. Even if you have fewer players, follow along to see how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor sits in the Cradle with warbands spread across two or three sites. The three Exiles have placed their pawns in the Hinterland and outer Provinces with just a few warbands each. The oath is Supremacy, meaning the Chancellor wins by controlling the most sites. The Chancellor already controls several, so the Exiles need to act fast.

walkthrough.title: Chancellor's First Turn
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor goes first. They collect supply based on their controlled sites, gaining several warbands and favor. Then they Muster, placing warbands on a key site in the Provinces to reinforce it. Finally, they Trade at a site to pick up a denizen as an adviser. The Chancellor is consolidating power early.

walkthrough.title: Exile One: The Impatient Attack
walkthrough.text: Exile One sees the Chancellor's warbands on a nearby Province site and decides to Campaign immediately. They spend most of their supply mustering warbands and then attack. But the Chancellor has more warbands and a defensive advantage. Exile One rolls poorly, loses most of their warbands, and now has almost nothing left. This was a costly mistake.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Attack Too Early
walkthrough.text: Attacking the Chancellor in round one is almost always a bad idea. The Chancellor starts with more resources, better defensive positions, and more warbands. Exiles need to build up their position before engaging in combat. Use the early rounds to gather advisers, accumulate warbands, and find vision cards. Strike when you have an advantage, not out of impatience.

walkthrough.title: Exile Two: Smart Opening
walkthrough.text: Exile Two takes a different approach. They Travel to a site with a useful denizen card, then Trade to take that denizen as an adviser. This adviser grants extra supply on future turns. Now Exile Two will generate more resources every round, compounding their advantage over time. This is exactly how an Exile should start.

walkthrough.title: Exile Three: Searching for a Vision
walkthrough.text: Exile Three uses their turn to Search the world deck. They draw several cards and find a vision card. This vision says they win if they control two specific sites and hold a relic. Now Exile Three has a clear goal to work toward. They keep the vision hidden for now, planning to play it at the right moment.

walkthrough.title: Why Visions Matter
walkthrough.text: Finding a vision card early is one of the best things an Exile can do. Without a vision, your only path to victory is seizing the oath from the Chancellor, which requires enormous military strength. A vision gives you a specific, achievable goal that might not require direct confrontation. Search early and often until you find one.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Exile One Recovers
walkthrough.text: After the disastrous attack, Exile One is in trouble. They have few warbands and little favor. This turn, they use Recover to regain some resources, then Travel to a less contested site in the Hinterland. Instead of attacking, they Trade for a cheap adviser. It is a humble turn, but it starts rebuilding their position.

walkthrough.title: The Chancellor Overextends
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor, feeling confident, decides to push into the Hinterland to control even more sites. They Travel far from the Cradle and Muster warbands at a distant site. But this leaves their core sites in the Provinces with fewer defenders. The Chancellor has spread too thin, and the Exiles notice.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Overextension Is Dangerous
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor's biggest weakness is trying to control everything. Every warband placed in the Hinterland is one fewer defending the Cradle or Provinces. As Chancellor, focus on holding what you need for the oath condition rather than grabbing every site. Quality of position matters more than quantity of territory.

walkthrough.title: Exile Two Makes a Deal
walkthrough.text: Exile Two approaches Exile Three with a proposal: let's both attack the Chancellor's weakened Province sites next round. Exile Three agrees, but secretly plans to betray the deal later once the Chancellor is weakened. This kind of negotiation and deception is core to Oath. No agreement is binding, and everyone has their own agenda.

walkthrough.title: Exile One's Mistake: Ignoring the Oath
walkthrough.text: Exile One has been rebuilding but has made a subtle error. They have been gathering advisers and warbands without any plan for how to actually win. They have no vision card, and they are not positioned to seize the oath from the Chancellor. Accumulating resources without a win condition in mind wastes precious rounds.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Have a Win Condition
walkthrough.text: Every action you take should move you closer to a win condition. As an Exile, either find a vision card to pursue or position yourself to seize the oath. As the Chancellor, protect the oath conditions. Playing without a plan leads to strong-looking board positions that cannot actually win the game. Ask yourself each turn: how does this move help me win?

walkthrough.title: Round Three: The Citizenship Gambit
walkthrough.text: Exile One, struggling to find a path to victory, offers to become a Citizen of the Chancellor's empire. The Chancellor accepts, gaining an ally. Now Exile One wins if the Chancellor wins. This gives Exile One a clear win condition without needing to fight anyone. But the other Exiles now face a stronger coalition.

walkthrough.title: Exile Three Plays Their Vision
walkthrough.text: Exile Three has been quietly building toward their vision's requirements. They play the vision card face up, revealing their win condition to the table. Now everyone knows what Exile Three needs. The Chancellor and Exile Two can try to block it, but Exile Three has a head start. Timing when to reveal your vision is critical.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Timing Your Vision
walkthrough.text: Playing a vision too early gives opponents many rounds to block you. Playing too late means you might not have time to fulfill it before round eight. The ideal time is when you are close to meeting the conditions and can achieve victory within one or two rounds. Watch the round track and plan accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Round Four: The Coordinated Strike
walkthrough.text: Exile Two campaigns against a weakened Chancellor site in the Provinces and takes control. Meanwhile, the Citizen moves warbands to help defend the Chancellor's remaining sites. The board is shifting dramatically. Sites change hands, warbands are lost, and the balance of power tilts. This is when Oath gets intense.

walkthrough.title: Exile One's Mistake: Blind Loyalty
walkthrough.text: As a Citizen, Exile One has been loyally supporting the Chancellor, spending all their resources to defend the empire. But round five is approaching, and the Chancellor is losing ground. Exile One could betray the Chancellor, return to Exile status, and pursue their own victory. Instead, they stay loyal out of habit. Sometimes the smart play is to abandon a sinking ship.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Citizenship Is a Tool, Not a Commitment
walkthrough.text: Becoming a Citizen is a strategic choice, not a permanent alliance. If the Chancellor is losing, betraying them and returning to Exile status lets you pursue your own win condition. The best Citizens keep one eye on an exit strategy. Use citizenship to gain resources and positioning, then betray when the time is right.

walkthrough.title: The Victory Check
walkthrough.text: Round five ends, and the first victory check occurs. The Chancellor counts their controlled sites for the Supremacy oath, but Exile Two has taken enough sites to deny the Chancellor victory. Exile Three checks their vision conditions but is one relic short. No one wins this round. The game continues to round six with rising tension.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues through rounds six, seven, and eight with victory checks at the end of each round. The pressure mounts as the Chancellor tries to reclaim lost sites, Exile Three races to complete their vision, and Exile Two plays kingmaker between them. Every action becomes critical as the game approaches its climax.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the core actions, learned how Exiles and the Chancellor approach the game differently, and watched several common mistakes play out. Remember: do not attack too early, always have a win condition, time your vision carefully, and treat citizenship as a strategic tool.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and role-specific advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to actions, suits, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Every action should advance a win condition
Exile: find vision or position to seize oath
Ask each turn: how does this help me win?

summary.title: Round Three: The Citizenship Gambit
bullets: Struggling Exile offers citizenship to Chancellor
Chancellor accepts — gains an ally
Citizen wins if Chancellor wins, but can betray later

summary.title: Exile Three Plays Their Vision
bullets: Vision played face up — win condition now public
Opponents can try to block, but Exile 3 has head start
Timing the reveal is critical

summary.title: Lesson: Timing Your Vision
bullets: Too early = opponents have rounds to block
Too late = not enough time to fulfill
Ideal: close to conditions, achievable in 1-2 rounds

summary.title: Round Four: The Coordinated Strike
bullets: Exile 2 campaigns and takes a Chancellor site
Citizen defends Chancellor's remaining positions
Board shifts dramatically — power balance tilts

summary.title: Exile One's Mistake: Blind Loyalty
bullets: Citizen spends all resources defending Chancellor
Chancellor losing ground — loyalty may be futile
Sometimes smart play is to betray and pursue own win

summary.title: Lesson: Citizenship Is a Tool, Not a Commitment
bullets: Citizenship = strategic choice, not permanent alliance
If Chancellor is losing, betray and go Exile
Best Citizens keep an exit strategy ready

summary.title: The Victory Check
bullets: Round 5 ends → first victory check
Chancellor denied Supremacy, Exile 3 one relic short
No winner — game continues with rising tension

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Rounds 6-8: victory checks each round, pressure mounts
Every action critical as game approaches climax
Chancellor, Exiles, and Citizen all racing for victory

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Core actions demonstrated, role strategies shown
Key lessons: don't attack early, have a win condition, time visions, use citizenship strategically
Ready to play your first full game

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → role-specific strategy advice
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for actions and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Oath players make. First, attacking the Chancellor too early when you lack the warbands to succeed. Second, playing without a clear win condition, accumulating power with no plan to use it. Third, ignoring the round track and getting surprised when victory checks begin at round five. Fourth, treating citizenship as permanent instead of a strategic tool you can abandon. Fifth, hoarding secrets or favor without converting them into board position. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Playing as the Chancellor
walkthrough.text: As Chancellor, your job is defense. You start with the most power, but everyone is gunning for you. Consolidate your position in the Cradle and key Provinces rather than expanding aggressively. Accept Citizens when offered, as they reduce your opponent count and share your victory. Know exactly what your oath requires and protect those conditions above all else. Use diplomacy to divide the Exiles against each other. A Chancellor who fights everyone at once will lose.

walkthrough.title: Playing as an Exile
walkthrough.text: As an Exile, your first priority is finding a path to victory. Search for a vision card in the early rounds to give yourself a specific goal. Without one, your only option is seizing the oath, which requires overwhelming military force. Build your adviser column for better supply generation. Form temporary alliances with other Exiles against the Chancellor, but remember that only one player can ultimately win. Consider citizenship as a fallback if the Chancellor looks unstoppable.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Advisers
walkthrough.text: Advisers are the engine of your position in Oath. A strong adviser column generates more supply each turn, provides combat bonuses, and grants special abilities. Prioritize acquiring advisers in the early rounds. Look for denizens with powers that complement your strategy. A player with three good advisers will consistently outperform a player with many warbands but no advisers, because supply generation compounds every single turn.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation Fundamentals
walkthrough.text: Oath is a negotiation game at its heart. Every interaction is an opportunity to bargain. Offer truces to players you do not want to fight right now. Threaten attacks to deter aggression. Propose alliances against the strongest player. Make promises you intend to break when it benefits you. The key principle is that your word has value only as long as others believe it. Build a reputation for reliability when it is cheap, so that when you betray someone at a critical moment, they do not see it coming.

walkthrough.title: Round Pacing and Timing
walkthrough.text: Oath has a natural rhythm. Rounds one through three are for building: gathering advisers, mustering warbands, searching for visions, and positioning on the map. Round four is the pivot point where you shift from building to execution. Rounds five through eight are the endgame, where every action must advance your win condition because victory checks happen each round. Do not waste early rounds on premature attacks, and do not waste late rounds on actions that do not move you toward winning.

walkthrough.title: Managing Warbands
walkthrough.text: Warbands are your most visible resource, and managing them is critical. You have a limited supply, so every warband placed on the map is one fewer in reserve. Concentrate your forces on sites that matter rather than spreading them across the entire map. When defending, position warbands where the oath requires you to hold. When attacking, mass enough warbands to guarantee success because a failed campaign wastes supply and leaves you vulnerable.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: Before each turn, take a moment to read the entire board. Count warbands on key sites. Check who holds the banners. Look at which denizens are available at sites you can reach. Consider what other players need to win and whether they are close to achieving it. The player who reads the board best will make the most informed decisions. If an opponent is one step from victory, everything else takes a back seat to stopping them.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't attack Chancellor too early — build first
Don't play without a win condition plan
Don't ignore round track — victory checks start round 5
Don't treat citizenship as permanent — it's a tool
Don't hoard resources without converting to board position

Accept Citizens to reduce opponents
Protect oath conditions above all else
Divide Exiles through diplomacy

summary.title: Playing as an Exile

Build adviser column for supply generation
Form temporary alliances vs. Chancellor
Consider citizenship as fallback if Chancellor is dominant

summary.title: The Power of Advisers
bullets: Advisers = engine (supply, combat, abilities)
Prioritize acquiring advisers in early rounds
Supply generation compounds every turn
3 good advisers > many warbands with no advisers

summary.title: Negotiation Fundamentals
bullets: Every interaction = bargaining opportunity
Truces, threats, alliances, promises — all valid
No deals binding — plan betrayals strategically
Build reliability reputation to make future betrayals effective

summary.title: Round Pacing and Timing
bullets: Rounds 1-3: build (advisers, warbands, visions, positioning)
Round 4: pivot from building to execution
Rounds 5-8: endgame, every action must advance win condition
Don't waste early rounds on attacks or late rounds on setup

summary.title: Managing Warbands
bullets: Limited supply — every placement is a tradeoff
Concentrate forces on sites that matter for oath/vision
Mass enough to guarantee success before attacking
Failed campaigns waste supply and leave you vulnerable

summary.title: Reading the Board State
bullets: Count warbands, check banners, scan available denizens each turn
Monitor opponents' proximity to their win conditions
If someone is 1 step from victory → stopping them is top priority
Best decisions come from complete board awareness

walkthrough.title: Chancellor: The Fortress Strategy
walkthrough.text: Stack warbands on the sites that satisfy your oath condition and let the Exiles waste resources attacking you. Accept Citizens to pad your alliance. The key insight is that you do not need to control the whole map. You just need to hold the oath condition from round five onward. Let the Exiles fight each other while you defend what matters.

walkthrough.title: Chancellor: Divide and Conquer
walkthrough.text: An aggressive Chancellor strategy is to actively pit Exiles against each other through negotiation and selective aggression. Attack the strongest Exile to keep them in check, then offer citizenship to a weakened Exile to gain an ally. Use your superior starting resources to make an example of one player early. The other Exiles will think twice before challenging you. This works best with four or more players where Exile coordination is harder.

walkthrough.title: Exile: The Vision Rush
walkthrough.text: Spend rounds one and two searching the world deck aggressively. Once you find a vision whose conditions align with your current position, quietly work toward fulfilling them. Play the vision card only when you are one round away from meeting the conditions. The surprise factor gives opponents minimal time to react. This strategy works best when the Chancellor is focused on defending against other threats.

walkthrough.title: Exile: The Military Overthrow
walkthrough.text: If you cannot find a good vision card, the alternative is to amass enough military power to seize the oath directly. This requires patience and careful warband management. Build your adviser column for maximum supply generation. Muster warbands steadily over several rounds. Form a temporary alliance with another Exile for a coordinated strike. Then launch a devastating campaign in round four or five, taking enough sites to deny the Chancellor their oath condition and seize it yourself.

walkthrough.title: The Citizenship Pivot
walkthrough.text: One of the most sophisticated strategies in Oath is the citizenship pivot. Become a Citizen early, gaining access to the Chancellor's resources and the Cradle. Use this position to build your own power while appearing loyal. Then, at the critical moment, betray the Chancellor and switch back to Exile status with a vision card ready. The timing must be precise: betray too early and you lack the resources, too late and the Chancellor has already won. This strategy is high-risk, high-reward.

walkthrough.title: Banner Control Strategies
walkthrough.text: When the oath involves banners, controlling them becomes the central strategic challenge. For People's Favor, acquire advisers in the most popular suit and control sites with matching denizens. Track which suits other players are collecting and try to dominate the most popular one. For Darkest Secret, accumulate secret tokens through trade actions and card powers. Both banners can shift rapidly, so timing your push to control them is crucial. Secure the banner in round four and defend it through the endgame.

walkthrough.title: Suit Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players understand that the six denizen suits shape the entire strategic landscape. If Beast cards dominate the world deck, Beast-related powers and People's Favor tracking favor Beast collectors. You can manipulate suit influence by trading for specific suited advisers, campaigning to remove opponents' suited cards, and using Search to find cards that complement your suit strategy. In campaign play, winning the game lets you curate the world deck, allowing you to shape future games around suits you prefer.

walkthrough.title: Kingmaking and Threat Assessment
walkthrough.text: In Oath, every player's actions affect who wins. If you cannot win yourself, you become a kingmaker, and deciding who wins can be a powerful position. Conversely, threat assessment is critical: identify who is closest to winning and focus on stopping them. The most dangerous player is rarely the one with the most warbands. It is the player quietly building toward a vision condition while everyone else fights over sites. Always ask: who wins if I do nothing this round?

walkthrough.title: Meta-Game Strategy (Campaign Play)
walkthrough.text: In a campaign where you play multiple games in sequence, the meta-game becomes crucial. Winning a game lets you shape the next one: choose the oath, curate the world deck, and set the map. Think about what gives you an advantage as Chancellor next game. Remove denizen cards that help strategies that counter yours. Choose an oath you know how to defend. Set up the map to favor your preferred approach. The best Oath players think two games ahead.

walkthrough.title: Relic Hunting
walkthrough.text: Relics are among the most powerful items in Oath, and savvy players use them to tip the balance. Relics are found by searching the world deck or accessing the reliquary. Each relic has a unique, game-changing ability. Some visions require holding specific relics, making them essential for certain win conditions. In campaign play, relics persist in the reliquary between games, so the winner can strategically place relics that benefit their future Chancellor position. Never ignore a relic opportunity, as even one can swing the entire game.

You don't need the whole map — just hold the oath condition

entries.definition: The player who rules the empire. Starts with the most resources and territory. Wins by fulfilling the oath condition at the end of rounds five through eight. If no one else wins by round eight, the Chancellor wins by default.
Any player not currently allied with the Chancellor. Exiles start with fewer resources and must find their own path to victory, either through vision cards or by seizing the oath.
An Exile who has sworn allegiance to the Chancellor. Citizens share the Chancellor's victory and gain access to the Cradle. They can betray the Chancellor and return to Exile status at any time.
An action that moves your pawn from one site to another. Movement costs supply, with greater distances costing more. Your pawn's location determines where you can perform other actions.
An action that places warbands from your supply onto the site where your pawn is located. Costs supply. Used to reinforce positions, prepare for attacks, or establish site control.

An action that lets you interact with denizen cards at your current site. You can take a denizen as an adviser by paying its favor cost, exchange favor for secrets, or use other trade-related abilities.

An action that initiates combat against warbands at your site or an adjacent site. Both sides roll dice based on warband count. If the attacker eliminates all defenders, they take control of the site.
An action that draws cards from the world deck. You may find denizen cards, relic cards, or vision cards. Found items can be played to sites, taken as advisers, or held for later use.
An action that restores spent resources. Regain favor, return warbands to supply, or retrieve cards. Essential for maintaining momentum across multiple rounds.
The resources gained at the start of each turn, consisting of warbands and favor. Supply amount depends on controlled sites and adviser powers. Supply fuels all actions on your turn.
The main currency in Oath. Used to pay for Trade actions, acquire advisers, and perform various game interactions. Represented by small tokens. Can be exchanged for secrets.
A hidden resource with powerful uses. The player holding the most secrets controls the Darkest Secret banner. Secrets can be spent for powerful effects and are gained through Trade and card powers.
Colored cubes representing military forces. Placed on sites to establish control and defend territory. Each player has a limited supply. Used in campaigns for attack and defense.

A denizen card placed in your adviser column on your player board. Advisers provide ongoing powers, increase supply, grant combat bonuses, and contribute suit influence. Building a strong adviser column is essential.

Cards representing characters, creatures, and locations in the world. Found at sites and in the world deck. Can be taken as advisers or placed on sites. Each belongs to one of six suits and has unique powers.

Powerful unique items found in the world deck or reliquary. Relics provide game-changing abilities and are sometimes required for vision conditions. Persist in the reliquary between games in campaign play.
Cards that provide alternative win conditions for Exiles. When played face up, the vision's conditions become public. Fulfilled at end of round to win. Found through the Search action.
The large deck of denizen and relic cards that players draw from when Searching. Its composition is determined by the previous game's outcome in campaign play, or by default for first games.
The card defining the Chancellor's primary win condition and the default victory condition. Four types: Supremacy, the People, Devotion, and Protection. Determined by the previous game's winner.
The Chancellor wins by controlling the most sites on the map. This is the default oath for first games. Exiles must capture sites to deny the Chancellor this condition.
The Chancellor wins by holding the People's Favor banner. Requires dominating the most popular denizen suit through advisers and controlled sites.
The Chancellor wins by holding the Darkest Secret banner. Requires accumulating the most secret tokens among all players.
The Chancellor wins by fulfilling a specific defensive condition tied to site control and banner status. The exact requirements depend on the game's setup.

At the end of rounds five through eight, all win conditions are checked. If the Chancellor holds the oath condition, they win. If an Exile fulfills a played vision, they win. Citizens win with the Chancellor. If no one wins by round eight, Chancellor wins by default.
The leftmost region on the map. The Chancellor's home territory and stronghold. Contains sites that are typically well-defended. Citizens gain access to the Cradle when they swear allegiance.
The middle region on the map. Contested territory between the Chancellor and Exiles. Contains sites with valuable denizens. Most combat occurs here.
The rightmost region on the map. The frontier where Exiles typically start. Contains sites that are farther from the Chancellor's power base but offer opportunities for building strength.
A location card placed in a region slot on the map. Sites hold denizen cards and warbands. Controlling a site (most warbands) provides supply and may satisfy oath or vision conditions.

A player controls a site by having the most warbands there. Control determines supply income, oath fulfillment, and denizen access. Control can shift when warbands are added or removed through campaigns.

A banner that goes to the player with the most influence in the most popular denizen suit. Tracked by counting suited advisers and controlled site denizens. Can be a win condition under the Oath of the People.
A banner that goes to the player holding the most secret tokens. Can be a win condition under the Oath of Devotion. Shifts as players gain and spend secrets throughout the game.
One of six denizen suits. Discord cards typically involve disruption, sabotage, and chaos-themed powers that hinder other players.
One of six denizen suits. Arcane cards involve mystical powers, secrets, and knowledge-themed abilities.
One of six denizen suits. Order cards involve structure, defense, and governance-themed powers that strengthen established positions.
One of six denizen suits. Hearth cards involve community, prosperity, and trade-themed powers that generate resources.
One of six denizen suits. Beast cards involve wild creatures, nature, and combat-themed powers that grant military advantages.
One of six denizen suits. Nomad cards involve travel, exploration, and movement-themed powers that improve mobility and search.

The record of the game state that carries forward between plays. Tracks which cards are in the world deck, which sites are on the map, what the oath is, and what relics are in the reliquary. Nothing is permanently destroyed.
A board that holds relic cards between games. Relics placed here persist into future games. The winner of each game can place relics in the reliquary as part of world deck curation.
After winning a game, the winner chooses which denizen and relic cards remain in the world deck for the next game. This shapes available strategies and suit composition in future plays.
Tracks the current round from one to eight. Victory conditions are checked at the end of rounds five through eight. The round marker advances at the end of each round.
Each player's personal board holding their adviser column, warband supply, and resources. Shows available actions and tracks your current state.
Your character piece on the map. Your pawn's location determines where you can muster, trade, campaign, and search. Moved via the Travel action.

When a Citizen breaks their allegiance to the Chancellor and returns to Exile status. The Citizen loses Cradle access but regains the ability to pursue independent victory conditions. Can be devastating if timed well.
A player's influence in a denizen suit, calculated from advisers and controlled site denizens of that suit. Determines who holds the People's Favor banner.
